description: Rules detectable by specific function words, modal keywords, negation markers, discourse connectives, conjunctions, and prepositions. Uses keyword/lemma lookup and keyword-anchored token patterns.

examples:
Will you be able to come this time? (Brazil; A2 WAYSTAGE; 2008; Portuguese; Pass)

[talking about contact lenses] Without them I will not be able to play football. (Spain; A2 WAYSTAGE; 2005; Spanish -
structures.explanation: Future ability — 'will be able to' expresses capability in the future (e.g. 'I will be able to help you tomorrow').

examples:
If I need a camera, I will use my mobile phone. (Turkey; A2 WAYSTAGE; 2007; Turkish; Pass)

And if my mum can, she will fetch you. (Italy; A2 WAYSTAGE; 2009; Italian; Pass)

If you can help me, I will
structures.explanation: Conditional modal — modal verb in the main clause of an 'if' sentence, describing a possible outcome (e.g. 'If you come, I'll be happy').

structures.guideword: FORM/USE: MID POSITION, HEDGING OR EMPHASIS
examples:
It is probably in the sitting room on the table. (Czech Republic; A2 WAYSTAGE; 2005; Czech; Pass)

You should probably wear a T-shirt and shorts, with sneakers. (Maldives; A2 WAYSTAGE; 2008; Others; P
structures.explanation: Modal adverb in mid-position — adverb placed between subject and main verb to soften or emphasise (e.g. 'I will probably go').

examples:
I'm sure I can beat you at chess. (Malaysia; A2 WAYSTAGE; 2007; Malay; Pass)

I am not sure what I want to do but I am sure we will find something to do. (Norway; A2 WAYSTAGE; 2003; Norwegian; Pass)
structures.explanation: 'Be sure' structure — expresses certainty or strong advice (e.g. 'Be sure to call me').

examples:
You must wear your sports shoes and you must also bring your racket! (Greek)

I must be back at 10.15. (French)
structures.explanation: Modal verb in a positive statement — use of can, could, will, would, should, must, etc. in affirmative form.

examples:
I think I left my phone there, maybe in your kitchen. (Italy; A2 WAYSTAGE; 2005; Italian; Pass)

Maybe we could visit that park you told me about or play at your house. (Mexico; A2 WAYSTAGE; 2003; Spa
structures.explanation: Modal verb inside a clause — modal verb appearing in a dependent or subordinate clause.

examples: [talking about a restaurant] But many local people dare not go to Lily, because it is too expensive. (Taiwan; B2 VANTAGE; 1997; Chinese; Pass)

The country's population is not likely to increase fast enough. (France; C2 MASTERY; 1993; French; Pass)

[talking about relatives] You are not obliged to invite them over. (Greece; C2 MASTERY; 1993;

You don't have to bring many things with you. (Greek, A2 WAYSTAGE, Pass, 2006)

About the transport, you do not have to worry as my daddy can drop us at the sports centre. (Malay, A2 WAYSTAGE, Pass, 2

I knew I may not meet him again. (Thailand; B1 THRESHOLD; 2008; Thai; Pass)

I realise that may not be the best way of resolving your problem. (Poland; B1 THRESHOLD; 2008; Polish; Pass) 

They may not

I like to wear a shirt and pants. It might not be very fashionable, it might look simple, but that's the way I like it. (Thai, B1 THRESHOLD, Pass, 2006)

I think that people watch too much TV these da
structures.explanation: Modal verb in negative — negated modal (can't, won't, shouldn't, etc.) to say something is not possible, allowed, or advisable.

examples:
Sarah, would you like to play tennis this evening? (Portuguese, A2 WAYSTAGE, Pass, 2008)

I'm going to a concert on Saturday. Would you like to go with me? (Croatian, A2 WAYSTAGE, Pass, 2009)

I want
structures.explanation: Modal question with 'like' — used to make polite invitations or ask about preferences (e.g. 'Would you like a coffee?').

examples:
Then, if you'd rather go with your friend, you ought to talk to your parents first, and tell them what you think about it. (France; B1 THRESHOLD; 2008; French; Pass)

I think that you ought to play te

It was only €70. It is pink. It looks very nice. My advice, you should buy this. (Turkish, A2 WAYSTAGE, Pass, 2007)

…on Monday, you will have to stay in this class for six hours so you should bring s
structures.explanation: Giving advice — modal verb recommending a course of action (e.g. 'You should see a doctor').

examples:
I was thinking of painting my bedroom blue. It would look awesome. (Bengali, A2 WAYSTAGE, Pass, 2006)

It would be fun there. (Taiwan; A2 WAYSTAGE; 2010; Chinese; Pass)
structures.explanation: Hypothetical — modal used for imaginary or unreal situations (e.g. 'I would love to travel more').

examples:
The concert starts at midnight but we have to go before then because we have got to buy our tickets. (Spanish - European, A2 WAYSTAGE, Pass, 2010)

You have to bring your swimming costume. (Spanish -

Nowadays, one has to have finished at least senior high school for any employer to consider hiring you. (Greek, C2 MASTERY, Pass, 2005)

The State Universities have a higher level of education than Pu
structures.explanation: Expressing obligation — modal indicating something is required (e.g. 'You must wear a seatbelt').

examples:
[talking about a mobile phone] It is very important to me because I must call my parents. (Spanish - Latin American, A2 WAYSTAGE)

I will be at school, so you must arrive at 3 pm. You must take your t

[talking about something the speaker has found] She took it and said we must go and find him. (Poland; B1 THRESHOLD; 2008; Polish; Pass)

Your parents must learn to let you live your own life … (Germa

Is it possible to use the prize next year or must I cancel my trip to India? (Swedish, B2 VANTAGE)

Finally, how much money must I bring with me? (French, B2 VANTAGE)

I like summer so much because the weather is hot and we needn't wear a jacket. (Greece; B2 VANTAGE; 2000; Greek; Pass) 

[talking about preparing for a party] As for the music, you needn't worry about
structures.explanation: Obligation and necessity — modal expressing both a requirement and its urgency (e.g. 'You have to submit it today').

examples:
You can wear anything you want. (A2 WAYSTAGE, 2008, Spanish - Latin American)

You can’t bring your mobile phone. (A2 WAYSTAGE, 2006, Spanish - European)

You cannot wear jeans or flip-flops. (A2 WAYS

May I be your guide while you stay here? (Brazil; B1 THRESHOLD; 2004; Portuguese; Pass)

May I borrow your bike?  (Thailand; B1 THRESHOLD; 2005; Thai; Pass)

I am in the basketball team of my school and tomorrow there is an important game. Could I take the class that I will miss on Saturday? (Spanish - Latin American, B1 THRESHOLD, Pass, 2006)

…my mother
structures.explanation: Permission — modal used to ask for or grant permission (e.g. 'Can I leave early?', 'You may go').

examples:
The park is a great place for picnic. It has lots of trees, a beautiful view and fresh air. You might need to bring some food and drink. (Turkish, B1 THRESHOLD, Pass, 2009)

I really don't know what y

This club has been opened for just two weeks. I mean we must see it. (Czech, B1 THRESHOLD)

[talking about a day out] We must do that again, don't you think?! (Spanish - Latin American, B1 THRESHOLD)

Tomorrow evening there is Laura Pausini's concert in Turin. Shall we go? (Italian, A2 WAYSTAGE, Pass, 2010)

Shall we go to a Korean restaurant after the film? (Korean, A2 WAYSTAGE, Pass, 2003)

I think we should meet at 2 pm, and my mother will take us there so don't worry. (Spanish - Latin American, A2 WAYSTAGE, Pass, 2008)

I think you should meet me around 4 pm at my house. (Thai, A2 WAYS

We could have dinner after that, if you want. (Egypt; A2 WAYSTAGE; 2003; Arabic - Egyptian; Pass)

If you want to visit an interesting place near Paris, you could go to Versailles. (France; A2 WAYSTAG
structures.explanation: Making a suggestion — modal proposing an action (e.g. 'We could try a different route').

examples:
[talking about a jazz concert] It would be very good if you came with me. (India; A2 WAYSTAGE; 2009; Oriya; Pass)

It would be nice to bring your new computer game and some clothes. (Cyprus; A2 WAYSTA
structures.explanation: Indirect suggestion with 'it would be' — softened suggestion structure (e.g. 'It would be great to meet up').

examples: My mother will get us there, and she will bring us to my house after too. (Brazil; A2 WAYSTAGE; 2008; Portuguese; Pass)
structures.explanation: Willingness and offers — 'will' expressing readiness or making an offer (e.g. 'I'll carry that for you').

examples:
I would prefer to go on Sunday, because on Sunday I have no school. (Malaysia; A2 WAYSTAGE; 2003; Chinese; Pass)

[talking about going to see a film] I'd prefer to see it tomorrow. (Uruguay; A2 WAYSTA
structures.explanation: Wishes and preferences — modal expressing what one wants or prefers (e.g. 'I'd rather stay home', 'I'd love to go').

examples:
We had to leave without you because the bus driver wouldn't wait. (===NONE===, B1 THRESHOLD, Pass, 2004)

[talking about a guitar] It was all that I wanted in life but my parents would not allow me to
structures.explanation: Past willingness — 'would' describing readiness or refusal in a past situation (e.g. 'He wouldn't help me').

examples:
Well, you should be bouncing up and down with joy (just kidding)! (Spain; B2 VANTAGE; 2004; Spanish - European; Pass)

These are the reasons that I believe we should be reading books instead of watchi
structures.explanation: 'Should be' + continuous — describes what is expected to be happening right now (e.g. 'You should be studying').

structures.guideword: FORM/USE: 'AS YOU KNOW', ORGANISING, MARKING SHARED KNOWLEDGE
examples:
I can't come to your wedding because, as you know, I live in England and on Thursday 18th July I'm going to take the FCE exam. (A2 WAYSTAGE; 2007; Polish; Pass)

As you know, my parents are leaving ho
structures.explanation: 'As you know' — signals information both speakers share, used to organise speech and avoid repetition.

examples:
So, what do you think? (Brazil; A2 WAYSTAGE; 2009; Portuguese; Pass)

[talking about not being able to make it to a party] I have got an appointment with my dentist.  So, I'm really sorry about that a
structures.explanation: 'So' as a connector — 'so' at the start of a turn to summarise, conclude, or introduce a topic shift.

examples:
I bought a shirt, because I haven't got any. (Spain; A2 WAYSTAGE; 2008; Spanish - European; Pass)

As regards close friends, I don't have many. (Argentina; B1 THRESHOLD; 2009; Spanish - Latin American
structures.explanation: Negative pronouns — 'nobody', 'nothing', 'nowhere' express total negation without using 'not' (e.g. 'Nobody came').

structures.guideword: FORM: 'NOT', NON FINITE AND ELLIPTED CLAUSES
examples:
But every time a small envelope arrived, I ripped it open as fast as I could. Not this time. (Germany; B1 THRESHOLD; 2008; German; Pass)

I would like to apologise for not being able to attend on Frid
structures.explanation: Negating a non-finite clause — 'not' before an infinitive or participle (e.g. 'She told me not to worry', 'Not knowing what to do...').

examples:
Neither you nor I would like that. (Sweden; B2 VANTAGE; 1993; Swedish; Pass)

We can neither eat nor drink during the lessons. (Poland; B2 VANTAGE; 2000; Polish; Pass)

I have neither climbed nor surf
structures.explanation: 'Neither … nor' — correlative structure negating two alternatives at once (e.g. 'Neither the food nor the service was good').

examples:
Never have I seen such a realistic and impressive film before. (Argentina; B2 VANTAGE; 2008; Spanish - Latin American; Pass)

Never had I thought that my parents would do this to me. (Netherlands; B2
structures.explanation: Fronted 'never' with inversion — 'never' moved to the front for emphasis, triggering subject-auxiliary inversion (e.g. 'Never have I seen such a mess').

examples:
Neither of us is liable in this case. (Czech Republic; B2 VANTAGE; 2007; Slovak; Pass)

Moreover, none of my friends are interested therefore there is no one I can talk to. (Japan; B2 VANTAGE; 1993; J
structures.explanation: 'Neither of / none of' + pronoun — negative quantifier before a pronoun referring to a group (e.g. 'None of them arrived').
